user_prompt: In `DiskStats`, add a check to see if a file `firmware.bin` exists when all the other statistics are refreshed.
In `lib\MicroSdCard` make a file `FirmwareUpdate.cpp` and a corresponding header file. It should have one public function `update_firmware()`

`update_firmware()` has an optional callback that is a progress report about every 500ms or longer, the callback has bytes-written and bytes-total. Do not bother to check if 500ms has passed until every 4096 bytes written. And make sure the WDT doesn’t trip inside it.
Make a `FirmwareUpdateView.cpp` and corresponding header inside `lib\FlyGuiViews`
It uses `sprite_fwupdate` in the top center of the view, avoiding the top bar.
There are two buttons, one bottom left and one bottom right, also tied to `BtnA` and `BtnC`
The bottom left button is the ok-go button, using `sprite_greencheckmark_50`
The bottom right button is the cancel button, using `sprite_canceldoor_50`
On boot, after `MicroSdCard` initializes successfully, before any bluetooth or wifi initialization
If the file `firmware.bin` appears on the card, show this dialog.

If the battery is not full-ish, do not show the go button, instead, show a message “To perform a firmware update, battery needs to be fully charged”. Otherwise, make it say “Do you want to update firmware?”

When the user clicks go, remove the cancel button, remove the go button, replace it with `sprite_hourglass_60_1`. Start the firmware update by calling `update_firmware()` (and registering the progress callback)
Inside that function, open the `firmware.bin` file, write it into the ota buffer/inactive partition.

The progress call back triggers a cycling of the hourglass graphic through `sprite_hourglass_60_1 sprite_hourglass_60_2 sprite_hourglass_60_3` and also please use code very similar to `void CloudUploadView::drawProgress()` and the new `lib\FlyGui\ProgressBar.cpp` to draw a progress update.

All of this needs to be done in the same thread, no servicing any other polls or pumps, not even the gui itself. We only need the draw calls for the hourglass, and that’s only two instances. The clock can appear frozen. The battery tracker and `Hotel` needs to not do anything at all.

When fully written, we check if it is valid using internal ota mechanisms, return from `update_firmware()` , with some sort of return code about how successful it is (if secure boot is on, this will verify if it is valid). if it is successful, mark the partition as the boot partition, and show the user a thumbs up `sprite_thumbsup_100` up top and confirmation message, and delete `firmware.bin` from the microSD card. Otherwise, use `sprite_xcircle_100` and say it failed. Any touch on the screen reboots.

As a rule, `lib\FlyGuiViews\FirmwareUpdateView.cpp` should not reference any internal ESP OTA functionality. And `lib\MicroSdCard\FirmwareUpdate.cpp` should avoid referencing any GUI related calls, as that should be handled by the callback and the return code.
agent_reply: - Added [FirmwareUpdate.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/FirmwareUpdate.cpp:36) / header with synchronous `update_firmware()` using ESP OTA APIs, 4096-byte write chunks, ~500ms progress callback checks, and WDT feeding.

- Added [FirmwareUpdateView.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/FirmwareUpdateView.cpp:53) / header with the prompt, battery gate, progress bar/hourglass cycling, success/failure result screens, and touch-to-reboot.
- Wired the view into boot before Wi-Fi/Bluetooth setup in [main.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:417).
- Plain `pio run` still fails because the default `thefly` env does not define `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL`; that appears pre-existing.
Note: `src/version.c` was already dirty before I started and remains dirty after the version-generation build script ran.

- Added [FirmwareUpdate.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/MicroSdCard/FirmwareUpdate.cpp:36) / header with synchronous `update_firmware()` using ESP OTA APIs, 4096-byte write chunks, ~500ms progress callback checks, and WDT feeding.

- Added [FirmwareUpdateView.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/FirmwareUpdateView.cpp:53) / header with the prompt, battery gate, progress bar/hourglass cycling, success/failure result screens, and touch-to-reboot.
- Wired the view into boot before Wi-Fi/Bluetooth setup in [main.cpp](C:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:417).
- Plain `pio run` still fails because the default `thefly` env does not define `BUILD_WITH_SECURITY_LEVEL`; that appears pre-existing.
Note: `src/version.c` was already dirty before I started and remains dirty after the version-generation build script ran.

user_prompt: In FirmwareUpdateView, there are two buttons on the bottom corners of the screen.
Make them just slightly less close to the left or right edge of the screen. Imagine the screen is divided into three even columns, and put the buttons in the center of the left or right columns
agent_reply: Done in [FirmwareUpdateView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/FirmwareUpdateView.cpp:25). The buttons are now centered in the left and right thirds of the 320px screen, so they land at `x=28` and `x=242` instead of hugging the corners.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`; build passed.

agent_reply: Do you want to update firmware?
or, if battery is not high enough:
To perform a firmware update, battery needs to be fully charged

user_prompt: also make sure every `.cpp` file inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` meet the guidelines at `developer-docs\code-layout-policy.md`, however, it is also important that their code layout look almost the same between them as they do kind of inherit from the same base classes sometimes
agent_reply: The common shape is now: constants/types/prototypes first, primary view/API methods next, supporting/drawing/formatting helpers later. I also kept the FlyGui view files visually aligned with each other by using the same lightweight section markers and putting helper blocks at the bottom.
- `src\version.c` build churn was restored; final status only shows the intended layout files.

commit_message: diagnostics, fixed a bug with speaker peak decay

user_prompt: point me at all the places where an intentional reset is about to happen
agent_reply: I found these whole-device intentional reset paths:
`requestRebootWithFlag(uint32_t flag)` sets `reset_magic` / `reset_flag`, waits 10 ms, then calls `ESP.restart()`. I did not find any current callers.
`reboot_after_reset_dialog()` logs “reset confirmation dismissed; rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`. Used after password reset and memory reset dialogs.
When firmware update view is in `Complete` state, touch press/release delays then `esp_restart()`.
If cloud upload completes but modal/gui is unavailable, it logs “rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`.
Exiting cloud scroll context reboots by policy after Wi-Fi/cloud use.
Exiting Wi-Fi scroll after Wi-Fi has started reboots by policy.
Cloud upload confirmation dismissed: logs, delays, `esp_restart()`.
Pairing confirmation dismissed: logs “rebooting to restart Bluetooth cleanly”, flushes serial, delays, `esp_restart()`.
One test intentionally expects a watchdog reset rather than directly calling restart:
Prints “WDT reset is expected” before running PBKDF without watchdog servicing.
I ignored ordinary “reset internal state” functions, FIFO resets, decoder resets, and `esp_task_wdt_reset()` because those are not whole-device resets.
I found these whole-device intentional reset paths:
`requestRebootWithFlag(uint32_t flag)` sets `reset_magic` / `reset_flag`, waits 10 ms, then calls `ESP.restart()`. I did not find any current callers.
`reboot_after_reset_dialog()` logs “reset confirmation dismissed; rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`. Used after password reset and memory reset dialogs.
When firmware update view is in `Complete` state, touch press/release delays then `esp_restart()`.
If cloud upload completes but modal/gui is unavailable, it logs “rebooting”, delays, then `esp_restart()`.
Exiting cloud scroll context reboots by policy after Wi-Fi/cloud use.
Exiting Wi-Fi scroll after Wi-Fi has started reboots by policy.
Cloud upload confirmation dismissed: logs, delays, `esp_restart()`.
Pairing confirmation dismissed: logs “rebooting to restart Bluetooth cleanly”, flushes serial, delays, `esp_restart()`.
One test intentionally expects a watchdog reset rather than directly calling restart:
Prints “WDT reset is expected” before running PBKDF without watchdog servicing.
I ignored ordinary “reset internal state” functions, FIFO resets, decoder resets, and `esp_task_wdt_reset()` because those are not whole-device resets.

user_prompt: use git diff, review how I added screen fades to places where I want the UI to appear at least responding to user input, sometimes masking a reboot, sometimes masking time consuming operations, and suggest other potential places
agent_reply: No blocking issues found in the fade additions.

The `PlaybackView::handleDelete()` addition is especially nice now that it also refreshes `DiskStats` after successful delete. That path can involve SD remove, disk-space scan, recording scan, and file-list repopulation, so a fade is a reasonable “I heard you” signal.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:220) `onclick_main_files()` before `scroll_view->populateFiles()`, especially if lots of files or security level 1 PIN path.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:236) `onclick_main_wifi()` before `scroll_view->populateWifi()`, if Wi-Fi list load/formatting can pause.

- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:247) smartphone/laptop connect buttons before `connect_to_bluetooth_host()`, since connection startup may feel dead before waiting view appears.

- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:430) `onclick_wifi_ap()` before AP/WebServer startup, because `WifiManager::startSoftAp()` plus `WebServer::init()` can be chunky.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:682) `onclick_ntp_sync()` before starting NTP, if setup can block before the waiting view.
- [ScrollView.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.cpp:906) Bluetooth host delete/unpair path, if `removeBond`/NVS save is perceptible.
Tiny style thought: if fades keep spreading, a named helper like `showBusyFeedback()` might make intent clearer than using `quickScreenFade()` directly everywhere. Not urgent.
No blocking issues found in the fade additions.

The `PlaybackView::handleDelete()` addition is especially nice now that it also refreshes `DiskStats` after successful delete. That path can involve SD remove, disk-space scan, recording scan, and file-list repopulation, so a fade is a reasonable “I heard you” signal.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:220) `onclick_main_files()` before `scroll_view->populateFiles()`, especially if lots of files or security level 1 PIN path.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:236) `onclick_main_wifi()` before `scroll_view->populateWifi()`, if Wi-Fi list load/formatting can pause.

- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:247) smartphone/laptop connect buttons before `connect_to_bluetooth_host()`, since connection startup may feel dead before waiting view appears.

- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:430) `onclick_wifi_ap()` before AP/WebServer startup, because `WifiManager::startSoftAp()` plus `WebServer::init()` can be chunky.
- [main_callbacks.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main_callbacks.cpp:682) `onclick_ntp_sync()` before starting NTP, if setup can block before the waiting view.
- [ScrollView.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ScrollView/ScrollView.cpp:906) Bluetooth host delete/unpair path, if `removeBond`/NVS save is perceptible.
Tiny style thought: if fades keep spreading, a named helper like `showBusyFeedback()` might make intent clearer than using `quickScreenFade()` directly everywhere. Not urgent.
